sentence: Subjective:
HPI: The patient, a 45-year-old male, presented with an ankle injury sustained during a basketball game with his 10-year-old son.
He landed awkwardly after being approached by his son, resulting in a twisted ankle that is now swollen and bruised on the outside.
He has been experiencing difficulty walking and instability in the ankle, which he attributes to the injury.
He recently restarted his workout routine and is concerned about disrupting his progress.
His son is doing well, enjoying basketball and playing with friends.
Past medical history: [NOT MENTIONED]
Review of systems: [NOT MENTIONED]
Current medications: [NOT MENTIONED]
Objective:
Vital signs: [NOT MENTIONED]
Physical exam: The patient has exquisite tenderness on the lateral side of the ankle, with tenderness also present on the medial deltoid region.
There is swelling on the lateral side, but no epidermolysis or skin break.
Capillary refill is brisk, and there is no malalignment.
The patient is unable to walk on the ankle due to pain and instability.
Test Results:
X-ray results show a displaced lateral malleolus fracture at the Weber C level, with no evidence of medial or posterior malleolar fractures.
Assessment / Problem List:
Assessment: The patient has a displaced lateral malleolar fracture, which requires surgical intervention to achieve healing.
Problem list: Lateral malleolar fracture; suspected instability; possible need for surgery.
Plan:
I prescribed meloxicam for pain and swelling management.
I recommended icing the ankle and elevating it regularly, particularly at work since the patient works from home.
I will provide crutches to aid in mobility.
The patient will need to undergo surgery to stabilize the fracture, which will likely take about three months to recover from.
I advise the patient to continue coaching his son's basketball team in April as planned, but to modify his activities to avoid exacerbating the injury.
